section.title: Rise Up and Help Us
section.sectionRationale: A communal lament of the sons of Korah, this psalm moves from confident rehearsal of God's past victories to bewildered protest at present defeat, refusing to explain the nation's suffering as deserved punishment. Paul later cites verse 22 in Romans 8:36, showing that unexplained suffering for God's sake belongs to the pattern of the whole people of God, joined to Christ.
section.thesis: Israel remembers how God alone won their fathers' battles, yet now suffers crushing defeat and reproach despite unbroken covenant loyalty, so the psalm entrusts this unexplained suffering to God's steadfast love rather than to any theory that the sufferers deserved it, a protest Paul takes up on behalf of all who suffer for Christ's sake.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the fathers' victories won by God alone, verses 1-3; present trust that God alone still saves, verses 4-8; the bewildering complaint of rejection and reproach, verses 9-16; the protest of unbroken covenant loyalty even unto slaughter, verses 17-22; and the final cry for God to wake and redeem, verses 23-26.

units.label: We have heard with our ears, O God
units.note: the fathers' victories, won by God alone

verses.text: We have heard with our ears, O God; our fathers have told us the work You did in their days, in the days of old.
With Your hand You drove out the nations and planted our fathers there; You crushed the peoples and cast them out.

For it was not by their sword that they took the land; their arm did not bring them victory. It was by Your right hand, Your arm, and the light of Your face, 
because You favored them.

units.label: You are my King, O God
units.note: present trust that God alone still saves

verses.text: You are my King, O God, who ordains victories for Jacob.
Through You we repel our foes; through Your name we trample our enemies.
For I do not trust in my bow, nor does my sword save me.
For You save us from our enemies; You put those who hate us to shame.
In God we have boasted all day long, and Your name we will praise forever. Selah

units.label: But You have rejected and humbled us
units.note: the bewildering complaint of present defeat

verses.text: But You have {{zanach|rejected}} and humbled us; You no longer go forth with our armies.
You have made us retreat from the foe, and those who hate us have plundered us.
You have given us up as sheep to be devoured; You have scattered us among the nations.
You sell Your people for nothing; no profit do You gain from their sale.
You have made us a reproach to our neighbors, a mockery and derision to those around us.
You have made us a byword among the nations, a laughingstock among the peoples.
All day long my disgrace is before me, and shame has covered my face,
at the voice of the scorner and reviler, because of the enemy, bent on revenge.

units.label: Yet for Your sake we face death all day long
units.note: unbroken covenant loyalty even unto slaughter

verses.text:
All this has come upon us, though we have not forgotten You 
or betrayed Your covenant.
Our hearts have not turned back; our steps have not strayed from Your path.
But You have crushed us in the lair of jackals; You have covered us with deepest darkness.
If we had forgotten the name of our God or spread out our hands to a foreign god,
would not God have discovered, since He knows the secrets of the heart?
Yet for Your sake we face death all day long; we are considered as sheep to be slaughtered.

units.label: Rise up; be our help
units.note: the final cry for God to wake and redeem

verses.text: Wake up, O Lord! Why are You sleeping? Arise! Do not reject us forever.
Why do You hide Your face and forget our affliction and oppression?
For our soul has sunk to the dust; our bodies cling to the earth.
Rise up; be our help! Redeem us on account of Your {{chesed|loving devotion}}.

zanach.gloss: To reject, spurn, or cast off. The psalmist accuses God of this action against His own people, a bold complaint that names the felt experience of abandonment without concluding that God has broken His covenant faithfulness.

chesed.gloss: Steadfast, covenant loving devotion, God's loyal love that binds Him to His promises. The psalm's final appeal rests entirely on this word, asking God to act consistently with His own covenant character rather than on any merit of the sufferers.

basic.context: This communal lament, sung by the whole nation rather than one person, remembers how God alone, not the sword, won the land for their fathers, yet now confesses a bewildering present defeat, plunder, and mockery among the nations despite the people's unbroken loyalty to the covenant. It ends by begging God to wake up and act for the sake of His own steadfast love.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm refuses the simple explanation that suffering always proves hidden sin. The psalmist insists Israel has not forgotten God or turned from His path, verses 17-18, so the suffering remains unexplained even as it is fully entrusted to God rather than resolved by human reasoning.
basic.originalLanguages: God is charged with having zanach, rejected or cast off, His people, verse 9, an unusually bold verb for a lament directed at God Himself. Yet the psalm's whole appeal in the final verse rests on chesed, God's own steadfast, covenant loving devotion, the very quality the psalmist insists God has not truly abandoned.
basic.typology: Verse 22, being counted as sheep for the slaughter while remaining faithful to God, is quoted directly by Paul in Romans 8:36 concerning those united to Christ, showing that unexplained suffering for God's sake belongs to the pattern of Christ's own people, who are nonetheless more than conquerors through Him who loved them, Romans 8:37.
basic.christ: Paul quotes Psalm 44:22 word for word in Romans 8:36 to describe the sufferings of those in Christ, immediately answering it with 'in all these things we are more than conquerors through Him who loved us,' Romans 8:37, showing that this psalm's unresolved lament finds its resolution not in an explanation but in the unbreakable love of God secured in Christ.
basic.god: God is shown as the true and only source of victory, whose past deeds the fathers told of, yet whose present silence and apparent sleep the psalmist dares to name honestly, all while still resting every hope on God's own covenant loving devotion rather than any explanation of the suffering.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 8:36-37 quotes this psalm's verse 22 directly, applying its unexplained suffering to those in Christ and answering it with the certainty of Christ's love. Isaiah 53:7 pictures the Servant led like a sheep to the slaughter. 2 Corinthians 4:8-11 echoes the same pattern of being given over to death for Jesus' sake without being forsaken.
basic.oneStory: Israel's honest protest, unexplained suffering held together with unbroken covenant loyalty and hope in God's steadfast love, anticipates the sufferings of Christ's own people, whom Paul describes in the very words of this psalm, yet who are more than conquerors through Him who loved them and gave Himself for them.

context.literary: A communal lament of the sons of Korah, moving from confident historical recital, verses 1-8, to bewildered complaint, verses 9-22, and finally to urgent petition, verses 23-26, without resolving the tension between covenant loyalty and present suffering.
context.historical: Likely composed after a national military defeat, perhaps during the divided monarchy or exile, when Israel suffered plunder and mockery among the surrounding nations despite no clear act of covenant unfaithfulness that would explain it.
context.canonical: Stands among the psalms of communal lament that give the whole people of God words for suffering that has no tidy explanation, a pattern the New Testament, especially Paul in Romans 8, explicitly recognizes as continuing in the experience of the church united to Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To give the covenant community honest words for suffering that is not the fruit of their own unfaithfulness, refusing simplistic retribution theology, while still directing every hope toward God's own steadfast covenant love rather than toward self-justification.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes one nation's specific experience of defeat and reproach; it prescribes as an abiding pattern the practice of honest lament that neither denies real suffering nor abandons covenant faithfulness and hope in God's chesed.

debates.question: Does this psalm allow that God's people can suffer deeply without personal or corporate sin being the direct cause?

views.label: The suffering described here is ultimately traceable to hidden covenant unfaithfulness, even if the psalmist cannot see it, since Scripture elsewhere ties national disaster to sin
views.case: Much of the Old Testament, especially Deuteronomy 28 and the historical books, does tie Israel's defeats to covenant unfaithfulness, so some readers assume unnamed sin must lie behind this crisis too.

views.label: The psalm explicitly denies personal or corporate unfaithfulness as the cause, verses 17-21, insisting the suffering is genuinely unexplained rather than deserved, and the New Testament treats exactly this kind of suffering, Romans 8:36, as belonging to faithful believers
views.case: The psalmist directly states 'we have not forgotten You' and invites God Himself, who knows the secrets of the heart, to verify this, refusing any explanation that blames the sufferers, a stance Paul later affirms is not merely permissible but expected for those united to Christ.

debates.bearing: Reading the suffering as genuinely unexplained, not covert punishment, is essential for the psalm's pastoral use and matches how Paul reads and applies it centuries later to Christians suffering for Christ's sake.

study.originalLanguages: God is charged with having zanach, rejected or spurned, His own people, verse 9, a startlingly direct verb of complaint. Yet the psalm's entire final appeal rests on chesed, God's covenant loving devotion, verse 26, the very attribute the psalmist trusts has not actually failed even though it feels absent.

typology.type: The faithful covenant people counted as sheep for the slaughter while remaining loyal to God, verses 17, 22
typology.fulfillment: Paul applies this exact verse to those united to Christ in Romans 8:36, showing Christ's people share this pattern of unexplained suffering, held fast by unshakeable love, Romans 8:37.

typology.type: Victory won by God's own hand rather than by sword, verses 3-7
typology.fulfillment: Christ's own victory over sin and death, won not by human strength but by the Father's power, and shared with all who trust in Him rather than their own resources.

study.christ: Romans 8:36 quotes Psalm 44:22 exactly, 'we are considered as sheep to be slaughtered,' applying this ancient communal lament directly to those united to Christ. Paul does not explain away the suffering; he answers it in the next verse with the certainty that 'in all these things we are more than conquerors through Him who loved us,' Romans 8:37, showing that Christ's own love, not a resolved explanation, is what finally answers this psalm's unresolved cry.
study.god: God is the sole giver of victory, celebrated in the fathers' history, yet also the one the psalmist dares to accuse of sleeping and hiding His face. The psalm holds together bold, honest complaint against God with unwavering trust in God's own covenant loving devotion as the only ground for hope.

crossReferences.note: Paul quotes Psalm 44:22 directly to describe the sufferings of those in Christ, then answers it with the certainty of Christ's love.
The Servant of the Lord is led like a sheep to the slaughter, the same image this psalm uses of the suffering covenant people.
Paul describes being given over to death for Jesus' sake yet never forsaken, echoing this psalm's pattern of faithful suffering.

study.oneStory: Israel's honest protest, refusing to explain away unexplained suffering yet refusing also to abandon covenant loyalty or hope in God's steadfast love, anticipates the sufferings of Christ's own people. Paul takes up this psalm's very words for believers united to Christ, and answers its unresolved cry with the certainty that nothing can separate them from the love of God in Christ Jesus.

quiz.q: According to verses 1-3, how did the fathers actually take the land?

options.text: Not by their own sword, but by God's right hand, arm, and favor.
options.note: The psalm insists victory came only through God's favor, not human military strength.

options.text: By forming a strong military alliance with neighboring nations.
options.note: The text explicitly denies human means; it credits God's hand and favor alone.

options.text: By their own superior weapons and numbers.
options.note: The psalm directly denies this, crediting God's arm and favor rather than the sword.

options.text: Through a peace treaty rather than conquest.
options.note: The land was taken through conquest, but the point of the text is that God, not the sword, gave the victory.

quiz.q: What does the psalmist insist about the nation's conduct despite present suffering, verses 17-18?

options.text: They have not forgotten God or betrayed His covenant, nor turned back from His path.
options.note: The psalm explicitly denies covenant unfaithfulness as the cause of the suffering.

options.text: They openly admit to having worshiped foreign gods.
options.note: The text explicitly denies this in verse 20; it is a hypothetical the psalmist rules out.

options.text: They acknowledge some minor lapses but nothing serious.
options.note: The psalmist claims total, not partial, faithfulness, inviting God Himself to verify it.

options.text: They are uncertain whether they have sinned or not.
options.note: The psalmist is not uncertain; he confidently asserts unbroken loyalty to the covenant.

quiz.q: Which verse of this psalm does Paul quote directly in Romans 8:36?

options.text: Verse 22, 'we are considered as sheep to be slaughtered.'
options.note: Paul applies this exact line to believers suffering for Christ's sake, answering it with assured love in verse 37.

options.text: Verse 3, about the land taken by God's hand.
options.note: Paul's quotation in Romans 8:36 is verse 22, concerning suffering, not the conquest of the land.

options.text: Verse 8, about boasting in God all day long.
options.note: This verse expresses praise, not the suffering language Paul quotes in Romans 8:36.

options.text: Verse 26, the final cry for redemption.
options.note: This is the psalm's closing plea, but the specific verse Paul quotes in Romans 8:36 is verse 22.

quiz.q: What does the psalmist ask God to do in the closing verses, 23 and 26?

options.text: Wake up, arise, and redeem them because of His loving devotion.
options.note: The psalm ends with an urgent plea for God to act, grounded entirely in His covenant loving devotion, chesed.

options.text: Explain clearly why the suffering has happened.
options.note: The psalm never asks for an explanation; it asks God to act, not to account for the suffering.

options.text: Grant them victory through a stronger army.
options.note: The plea is for God Himself to arise and redeem, not for greater military strength.

options.text: Remove all future possibility of suffering.
options.note: The specific request is immediate help and redemption now, not a guarantee against all future suffering.

quiz.q: What is this psalm's overall stance toward the nation's suffering?

options.text: It is an honest protest that refuses to explain the suffering as deserved punishment, entrusting it instead to God's steadfast love.
options.note: The psalm holds bold complaint and unwavering covenant trust together without resolving why the suffering has come.

options.text: It calmly assumes the suffering must be deserved punishment for hidden sin.
options.note: The psalm explicitly denies this explanation in verses 17-21.

options.text: It abandons faith in God altogether because of the suffering.
options.note: The psalm never abandons faith; its entire appeal rests on God's covenant loving devotion.

options.text: It treats the suffering as unimportant and moves on quickly.
options.note: The suffering is described at painful length, verses 9-22, and taken with total seriousness.

questions: The psalmist remembers God's past faithfulness even while confessing bewildering present suffering. What past evidence of God's faithfulness do you return to when your present circumstances feel confusing or painful?

This psalm refuses to explain suffering as always being deserved punishment. Have you ever assumed, wrongly, that someone's suffering, or your own, must be traceable to hidden sin? How does this psalm's honesty challenge that assumption?

The psalmist boldly tells God 'You have rejected and humbled us' while still trusting His covenant love. What would it look like for you to bring an equally honest complaint to God without abandoning your trust in Him?

Paul quotes this psalm's verse 22 for Christians suffering for Christ's sake, answering it with assured victory in Romans 8:37. How does knowing that unexplained suffering can still be lived within the love of Christ change how you carry your own hardships?
The psalm ends with an urgent, unresolved cry, 'Rise up, be our help.' What unresolved situation in your life most needs that same honest, hope-filled cry today?

translationNotes.note: The light of Your face is a Hebrew idiom for God's favorable, personal presence, contrasted here with mere military strength as the true source of the fathers' victories.
Rejected renders zanach, a strong verb of spurning or casting off, an unusually direct accusation for a lament addressed to God Himself.
Sheep to be slaughtered is quoted word for word by Paul in Romans 8:36, applying this ancient communal lament to the sufferings of those united to Christ.
Loving devotion renders chesed, God's steadfast covenant love, the sole ground on which the psalm's final appeal for redemption rests.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 44:1-26. No em or en dashes present. Searched gospelinlife.com, truthforlife.org, desiringgod.org, and thegospelcoalition.org this session for a dedicated Psalm 44 sermon from the trusted cluster; none found, so sermon status is none.
